paper_title: The Exposure of U.S. Manufacturing Industries to Exchange Rates
research_answer: U.S. export sectors vary dramatically in their sensitivity to dollar appreciation. Motor vehicles are the most exposed, with an elasticity of -1.86 — meaning a 10% dollar appreciation reduces motor vehicle export volumes by nearly 19%. Aluminum and iron & steel exports also have elasticities greater than 1 in absolute value, making them highly sensitive. Wood and pulp exports have an elasticity of -1.27, also above unity. Paper exports are moderately sensitive at -0.63. By contrast, pharmaceuticals, chemicals, and plastics exports show no statistically significant response to exchange rate movements at all. This divide likely reflects product sophistication: pharmaceuticals (with a product complexity ranking of 234) are differentiated, hard-to-substitute goods where buyers don't easily switch based on price, while commodities like aluminum and steel face much more price-driven competition.
supporting_quotes: Motor vehicle exports have an exchange rate elasticity of -1.86, indicating that a 10 percent appreciation of the dollar will reduce the volume of exports by almost 19 percent.
Aluminum and iron & steel exports also have elasticities greater than unity.
Forestry exports are sensitive to the exchange rate, with the elasticity for wood & pulp exports equal to -1.27
and the elasticity for paper exports equal to -0.63
On the other hand, there is no evidence that pharmaceuticals, chemicals, and plastics exports are affected by exchange rates.
